questions.q: What is Kamala Sohonie best known for being the first Indian woman to achieve?
options: The first Indian woman to win a Nobel Prize
The first Indian woman to earn a PhD in a scientific discipline
The first Indian woman to lead ISRO
The first Indian woman in space
questions.explanation: Kamala Sohonie became the first Indian woman to be awarded a PhD in a scientific field.

questions.q: In which branch of science did Kamala Sohonie primarily work?
questions.explanation: Kamala Sohonie was a biochemist who studied nutrition and the chemistry of foods.

questions.q: Kamala Sohonie was a woman of which nationality?
questions.explanation: Kamala Sohonie was an Indian scientist, celebrated as a pioneer for women in Indian science.

questions.q: Sohonie studied the nutritional value of which traditional Indian drink?
questions.explanation: She investigated neera, the sweet sap from palm trees, and showed it was a cheap, nutritious dietary supplement.

questions.q: What major obstacle did Kamala Sohonie famously have to overcome to begin her research career?
options: A lack of any school education
Initial refusal of admission because she was a woman
questions.explanation: She was initially denied admission to the Indian Institute of Science simply because she was a woman, and had to fight for the opportunity.

questions.q: Kamala Sohonie's pioneering achievement most directly opened doors for which group?
questions.explanation: Her success helped break the barrier that had kept women out of advanced scientific research institutions in India.

questions.q: Which prestigious Indian research institution did Kamala Sohonie apply to as a young woman seeking to do research?
options: All India Institute of Medical Sciences
questions.explanation: She sought admission to the Indian Institute of Science in Bangalore, then headed by C. V. Raman.

questions.q: Kamala Sohonie's work largely focused on which broad area of human health?
questions.explanation: Much of her research dealt with the nutritional content of foods relevant to ordinary Indians.

questions.q: In which century did Kamala Sohonie carry out her pioneering scientific work?
questions.explanation: Sohonie lived from 1911 to 1998 and did her landmark research in the 20th century.

questions.q: What is the most fitting description of Kamala Sohonie's legacy in Indian science?
options: A trailblazer who opened the field of research to women
An astronaut who flew several missions
A founder of India's nuclear program
questions.explanation: She is remembered as a trailblazer whose persistence opened the door to scientific careers for Indian women.

questions.q: Which famous physicist and Nobel laureate, then director of IISc, initially refused Sohonie admission?
questions.explanation: Nobel laureate C. V. Raman initially refused her a place because she was a woman, before relenting under conditions.

questions.q: Roughly when was Kamala Sohonie born?
questions.explanation: Kamala Sohonie was born on 18 June 1911.

questions.q: At which renowned British university did Kamala Sohonie pursue her doctoral research?
questions.explanation: She carried out her doctoral research at the University of Cambridge.

questions.q: Sohonie's Cambridge research established that the enzyme cytochrome c is present in which kind of cells?
options: All plant cells (every plant tissue she examined)
questions.explanation: Her doctoral work demonstrated that cytochrome c, vital to cellular respiration, is present in all plant cells she examined.

questions.q: Which condition did C. V. Raman attach when he finally allowed Sohonie to join IISc?
options: She had to pay double tuition
She was admitted on probation and had to prove she would not distract male students
She had to change her field to physics
She had to study abroad first
questions.explanation: Raman admitted her only on a probationary basis with strict conditions, partly fearing a woman would distract the male researchers.

questions.q: Under which Nobel Prize-winning biochemist did Sohonie work during her time at Cambridge?
questions.explanation: At Cambridge she worked in the laboratory associated with Frederick Gowland Hopkins, a Nobel laureate in physiology or medicine.

questions.q: Sohonie's research on neera was undertaken partly to improve the diets of which population?
options: Malnourished and poor communities, including children and pregnant women
questions.explanation: She promoted neera as an inexpensive nutritional supplement to help combat malnutrition among poor and vulnerable groups.

questions.q: Where did Kamala Sohonie complete her undergraduate education before entering research?
questions.explanation: She graduated from the University of Bombay, topping her class in chemistry and physics.

questions.q: In which year did Kamala Sohonie pass away?
questions.explanation: Kamala Sohonie died in 1998.

questions.q: Which institute did Sohonie later serve as director, becoming the first woman to head it?
questions.explanation: She became the first woman director of the Royal Institute of Science in Bombay (Mumbai).

questions.q: Sohonie's father and uncle, who influenced her interest in science, were associated with which field?
questions.explanation: Her father and uncle were both chemists, and her family's scientific background encouraged her own pursuit of chemistry.

questions.q: What was a notable feature of the duration of Sohonie's PhD thesis at Cambridge?
options: It took over a decade to complete
It was remarkably concise, reportedly only around 40 pages
It was written entirely in Latin
questions.explanation: Her doctoral thesis was famously brief, reported to be roughly 40 pages, yet scientifically significant.

questions.q: Cytochrome c, the protein central to Sohonie's Cambridge work, plays a key role in which cellular process?
options: The electron transport chain of cellular respiration
questions.explanation: Cytochrome c is an electron carrier in the respiratory electron transport chain, essential for energy production in cells.

questions.q: Before going to Cambridge, Sohonie did early research on the proteins of which staple food in India?
questions.explanation: Her early biochemical work in India examined the proteins of milk, pulses and legumes important to Indian diets.

questions.q: Sohonie's work on neera was reportedly encouraged by which figure connected to Indian public life?
questions.explanation: Her neera research is associated with the encouragement of President Rajendra Prasad, reflecting its importance for nutrition.

questions.q: Which national civilian honour related to scientific and public service did Sohonie receive late in her career?
options: The Rashtrapati Award (recognition for her contributions)
questions.explanation: She received the Rashtrapati Award in recognition of her scientific contributions; she never held the Bharat Ratna.

questions.q: What broader change in IISc admissions is credited to Sohonie's persistence and proven ability?
options: The institute admitted women as research students thereafter
The institute closed its chemistry department
questions.explanation: After she proved herself, IISc began admitting women as regular research students, a lasting change to its policy.

questions.q: Sohonie's biochemical research career is best characterised as combining laboratory science with what social aim?
options: Improving public nutrition and food affordability
questions.explanation: She applied biochemistry to practical problems of nutrition, aiming to make affordable, nutritious food available to ordinary people.

questions.q: Sohonie's married surname 'Sohonie' came after marriage; what was she born with as a Maharashtrian name connecting her to a scientific family?
questions.explanation: She was born Kamala Bhagvat into a Maharashtrian family of chemists and took the name Sohonie after marriage.

questions.q: Which statement best summarises the scientific significance of Sohonie's Cambridge thesis finding?
options: It proved plants do not respire
It showed a respiratory enzyme thought specific to animals also functions in plant tissues
It discovered a new chemical element
It disproved the existence of enzymes
questions.explanation: By finding cytochrome c in plant tissues, she demonstrated that this respiratory enzyme is common to plant and animal cells alike.
